steps.title: Analyze function I: behavior of $f(x) = -6(3)^x - 3$
steps.content:
For exponential functions $a \cdot b^x + c$ where $a < 0$ and $b > 1$, the negative coefficient reverses the standard behavior.

Here, as $x$ increases, $(3)^x$ grows, so $-6(3)^x$ becomes increasingly negative, making $f(x)$ strictly decreasing.

As $x \to -\infty$, we have $(3)^x \to 0$, so $f(x) \to -6(0) - 3 = -3$.

This means $y = -3$ is a horizontal asymptote that the function approaches from below but never reaches, and since the function continues decreasing toward $-\infty$ as $x$ increases, there is no minimum value.

steps.title: Analyze function II: behavior of $g(x) = -3(6)^x$
steps.content:
Similarly, $g(x) = -3(6)^x$ has a negative coefficient and a base greater than 1, so it is also strictly decreasing.

As $x \to -\infty$, $(6)^x \to 0$, giving $g(x) \to -3(0) = 0$.

The function has a horizontal asymptote at $y = 0$ but again approaches it without reaching it, and the function decreases without bound as $x$ increases, so there is no minimum value.

steps.title: Conclusion: Neither function has a minimum value
steps.content:
For a function to have a minimum value equal to $-3$, it must actually attain that value at some point and have no lower output.

Both $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ are monotonically decreasing exponential functions that approach horizontal asymptotes but never reach minimum values—they extend to $-\infty$.

Function I approaches $-3$ from below but never equals it, and Function II approaches $0$ from below.

Neither has a minimum value at $-3$ or anywhere else. That matches choice D — D is correct.
